---
title: "Real Income vs Housing Prices in Singapore"
subtitle: "Did income growth keep pace with HDB resale prices, 2000–2025?"
author:
  - name: Shina Shih Xin Rong (2402781)
  - name: Jeanie Cherie Chua Yue-Ning (2403083)
  - name: Claudia Yue Xin Ying (2401817)
  - name: Nurul Zahirah Binte Muhamadnoh (2401671)
  - name: Anushka Chourasia (2503769)
date: "2026-03-22"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: false
    theme: cosmo
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r setup}
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(scales)

tidy_data <- readRDS("tidy_data.rds")

gap_data <- tidy_data %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Measure_Type, values_from = Index_Value) %>%
  mutate(Affordability_Gap = `HDB Resale Price` - `Real Income`)

col_measure <- c("Real Income" = "#2166AC", "HDB Resale Price" = "#B2182B")
col_group   <- c("Bottom 20%" = "#D73027", "Middle 60%" = "#FC8D59", "Top 20%" = "#4575B4")

base_theme <- theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14, margin = margin(b = 6)),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 10, color = "grey40", margin = margin(b = 12)),
    plot.caption     = element_text(size = 8, color = "grey55", hjust = 0,
                                    margin = margin(t = 10)),
    plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "#FAFAFA", color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#FAFAFA", color = NA),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90"),
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    legend.key.width = unit(1.5, "cm"),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12, margin = margin(b = 6)),
    axis.text        = element_text(color = "grey30"),
    axis.text.x      = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 9)
  )

events <- tibble(
  xmin  = c(2008, 2020),
  xmax  = c(2009, 2021),
  label = c("GFC", "COVID-19")
)
```

---

## Original Visualisation

The original visualisation — *"Average Household Income Per Household vs Purchasing Power Index (PPI)"* — was sourced from Stacked and presents nominal household income across ten deciles alongside a general Purchasing Power Index on a dual axis, covering 2010–2020.

![Original visualisation: Average Household Income Per Household vs PPI](Average-Household-Income-Per-Household-Image.jpg){width=85% fig-align="center"}
`
---

## Weaknesses of the Original Visualisation

**1. Dual-axis scale distortion.**
The original chart plots household income in SGD on the left axis and the Purchasing Power Index on the right axis, with each axis scaled independently. This allows the two series to be visually aligned at arbitrary points, creating a false impression of correlation or divergence that is an artefact of axis scaling rather than a property of the data. Readers cannot reliably judge whether income and purchasing power are actually moving together or apart.

**2. Ten income deciles cause visual congestion.**
Displaying all ten decile lines simultaneously produces a dense tangle of overlapping curves rendered in similar colours. The legend requires eleven entries (ten deciles plus PPI), forcing constant cross-referencing between legend and chart. No single income group's trajectory can be followed clearly, and distributional differences between groups are obscured rather than revealed.

**3. Nominal income without inflation adjustment.**
The original presents raw SGD figures unadjusted for inflation. Because all nominal values tend to grow over time, the chart systematically overstates real income gains. A household whose income doubled in nominal terms but faced equivalent CPI growth experienced zero improvement in purchasing power — a distinction the original chart cannot convey.

**4. No explicit affordability metric linking income to housing costs.**
The Purchasing Power Index reflects a general consumption basket. It does not connect income growth to the specific cost pressure most financially consequential for Singapore households — housing. A reader cannot determine from the original chart whether any income group can actually afford an HDB resale flat, nor how that affordability has changed over time.

**5. Dense legend and high cognitive load.**
Eleven legend entries combined with the dual-axis structure require substantial reader effort before any analytical insight can be extracted. The chart prioritises completeness over communicability.

---

## Improvements in the Revised Visualisation

**1. Indexed comparison removes dual-axis distortion.**
Both real income and the HDB resale price series are converted to a common index rebased to 2010 = 100. All series share one y-axis and one scale. Any visual divergence between lines reflects a genuine difference in growth rates — there is no axis manipulation possible. A gap of 30 index points means exactly that, regardless of the absolute income levels underlying it.

**2. Three aggregated income groups reduce cognitive load.**
The ten deciles are consolidated into Bottom 20%, Middle 60%, and Top 20%. This reduces the number of series from ten to four (three income groups plus HDB) while preserving the distributional contrast directly relevant to the research question. The legend shrinks to two entries per panel.

**3. Faceted layout separates groups without sacrificing comparability.**
Each income group occupies its own panel with an identical y-axis scale. The HDB resale price line is repeated identically across all three panels, so the reader can compare how far housing costs have outpaced each group's income without needing to visually untangle crossing lines. Cross-group comparison is immediate: the reader sees at a glance whether the red line pulls further above the blue line in the Bottom 20% panel than in others.

**4. Real income deflated by group-specific CPI.**
Nominal income is deflated using the CPI series specific to each household income group rather than a single all-items index. This reflects the empirical reality that lower-income households face different price pressures than higher-income households, making the income series a genuine measure of purchasing power rather than a nominal proxy.

**5. Shaded ribbon makes the affordability gap an explicit visual object.**
The area between the income and HDB lines is filled as a shaded band. Rather than asking the reader to mentally estimate the distance between two lines, the chart presents the gap directly as a visible, named region that grows or shrinks over time. This transforms an implied comparison into an explicit affordability metric.

**6. Direct endpoint labels replace axis-reading.**
The 2025 index values for both series are annotated directly on each panel. The key quantitative finding — the gap between the two series at the end of the study window — is immediately legible without requiring the reader to trace a value back to the y-axis.

**7. Event annotations are horizontal and untruncated.**
The GFC (2008–2009) and COVID-19 (2020–2021) periods are marked with light vertical bands and clearly readable labels. This replaces the rotated, partially truncated text found in other versions of this chart and allows the reader to connect the affordability trajectory to known economic events without interrupting the flow of reading.

---

## The Improved Visualisation

```{r main-chart, fig.width=13, fig.height=6}
labels_2025 <- tidy_data %>%
  filter(Year == 2025) %>%
  mutate(label = paste0(round(Index_Value, 0)))

p <- ggplot(tidy_data,
            aes(x = Year, y = Index_Value, color = Measure_Type,
                linetype = Measure_Type)) +

  # Shaded gap ribbon
  geom_ribbon(
    data = gap_data,
    aes(x = Year, ymin = `Real Income`, ymax = `HDB Resale Price`),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    fill = "#B2182B", alpha = 0.07
  ) +

  # Event shading
  geom_rect(data = events,
            aes(xmin = xmin, xmax = xmax, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),
            inherit.aes = FALSE,
            fill = "grey50", alpha = 0.10) +

  # Event labels — rotated, inside grey band
  geom_text(data = events,
            aes(x = (xmin + xmax) / 2, y = 58, label = label),
            inherit.aes = FALSE,
            size = 2.5, color = "grey35", fontface = "italic",
            angle = 90) +

  # Base year reference line
  geom_hline(yintercept = 100, linetype = "dashed",
             color = "grey55", linewidth = 0.45) +

  # Main lines
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(data = tidy_data %>% filter(Year %% 5 == 0),
             size = 2.2, shape = 21, fill = "white", stroke = 1.1) +

  # 2025 endpoint labels
  geom_text(
    data = labels_2025,
    aes(label = label),
    hjust = -0.2, size = 3.2, fontface = "bold",
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +

  facet_wrap(~Income_Group, ncol = 3) +

  scale_color_manual(values = col_measure, name = NULL) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("Real Income"      = "solid",
                                   "HDB Resale Price" = "solid"),
                        name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2000, 2025, by = 5),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.14))) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(60, 180, by = 20),
    limits = c(55, 185),
    sec.axis = sec_axis(~ ., breaks = 100,
                        labels = "← base year (2010 = 100)")
  ) +

  labs(
    title    = "Real Household Income vs HDB Resale Price Index (2010 = 100)",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Singapore, 2000–2025  |  Income deflated by group-specific CPI  |  ",
      "Shaded band = affordability gap  |  Labels show 2025 index values"
    ),
    x       = NULL,
    y       = "Index value (2010 = 100)",
    caption = paste0(
      "Sources: Department of Statistics Singapore — Average Monthly Household Employment Income by Decile; ",
      "CPI by Household Income Group (2024 base year).\n",
      "Housing & Development Board / data.gov.sg — HDB Resale Flat Prices (transaction records, 2000–2025).\n",
      "Note: Income groups deflated using their respective group-specific CPI. ",
      "HDB index computed as annual median resale price, rebased to 2010 = 100."
    )
  ) +

  base_theme +
  theme(
    legend.text        = element_text(size = 11),
    legend.spacing.x   = unit(0.4, "cm"),
    axis.text.y.right  = element_text(size = 8, color = "grey50",
                                      face = "italic", hjust = 0),
    axis.ticks.y.right = element_line(color = "grey55")
  )

p
```

**Reading the chart.** Each panel shows one income group. The blue line tracks real (inflation-adjusted) household income; the red line tracks HDB resale prices. Both are indexed to 2010 = 100, so the lines share a common baseline — any divergence directly reflects a change in relative affordability. The shaded band between the lines is the affordability gap: when red rises above blue, housing is outpacing income.

---

## Key Insights

**Before 2010**, real income broadly kept pace with or exceeded HDB resale prices across all three income groups, reflecting relative affordability stability in the early 2000s.

**From 2010 onward**, resale prices diverge upward. The gap widens progressively, then accelerates sharply after 2020 — coinciding with the post-COVID resale surge driven by demand brought forward from the pandemic period.

**By 2025**, the HDB index reaches 171.6 — its highest level in the 25-year window — while real income indices are 140.5 (Bottom 20%), 141.8 (Middle 60%), and 113.8 (Top 20%). The affordability gap is largest in absolute index-point terms for the Top 20% (57.8 points), but this reflects real income plateauing under CPI deflation rather than greater financial stress. **The Bottom 20% gap (31.1 points) is the most policy-relevant**: their lower absolute income provides far less buffer against rising prices, and their real income growth in 2020–2025 is the weakest of all three groups.

---

## Supporting Analysis

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
### Affordability gap over time

```{r gap-chart, fig.width=10, fig.height=5.5}
gap_labels_2025 <- gap_data %>%
  filter(Year == 2025) %>%
  mutate(
    label = paste0(Income_Group, ": +", round(Affordability_Gap, 1), " pts"),
    y_pos = case_when(
      Income_Group == "Top 20%"    ~ 59,
      Income_Group == "Bottom 20%" ~ 32,
      Income_Group == "Middle 60%" ~ 26
    )
  )

ggplot(gap_data, aes(x = Year, y = Affordability_Gap, color = Income_Group)) +
  geom_rect(data = events,
            aes(xmin = xmin, xmax = xmax, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),
            inherit.aes = FALSE, fill = "grey50", alpha = 0.10) +
  geom_text(data = events,
            aes(x = (xmin + xmax) / 2, y = -39, label = label),
            inherit.aes = FALSE,
            size = 2.5, color = "grey35", fontface = "italic", angle = 90) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 1.8) +
  geom_text(
    data = gap_labels_2025,
    aes(y = y_pos, label = label),
    x = 2026, hjust = 0, size = 3, fontface = "bold",
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = col_group, name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = seq(2000, 2025, by = 5),
    limits = c(1999, 2033),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(-40, 80, by = 20),
    limits = c(-42, 85)
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Affordability gap by income group",
    subtitle = "Gap = HDB index − Real income index  |  Positive = housing outpacing income",
    x = NULL, y = "Gap (index points)",
    caption  = "Sources: SingStat, HDB / data.gov.sg"
  ) +
  base_theme
```
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
### CAGR by sub-period

```{r cagr-chart, fig.width=11, fig.height=5}
cagr_fn <- function(end_val, start_val, n) round(((end_val / start_val)^(1 / n) - 1) * 100, 2)

periods <- list(c(2000, 2010), c(2010, 2020), c(2020, 2025))

income_cagr <- map_dfr(periods, function(p) {
  tidy_data %>%
    filter(Measure_Type == "Real Income", Year %in% p) %>%
    pivot_wider(names_from = Year, values_from = Index_Value) %>%
    mutate(
      CAGR       = cagr_fn(.[[4]], .[[3]], p[2] - p[1]),
      Sub_Period = paste0(p[1], "–", p[2]),
      Measure    = "Real Income"
    ) %>%
    select(Income_Group, Sub_Period, Measure, CAGR)
})

hdb_cagr <- map_dfr(periods, function(p) {
  vals <- tidy_data %>%
    filter(Measure_Type == "HDB Resale Price", Year %in% p) %>%
    distinct(Year, Index_Value) %>%
    pivot_wider(names_from = Year, values_from = Index_Value)
  tibble(
    CAGR       = cagr_fn(vals[[2]], vals[[1]], p[2] - p[1]),
    Sub_Period = paste0(p[1], "–", p[2]),
    Measure    = "HDB Resale Price"
  )
}) %>%
  crossing(Income_Group = c("Bottom 20%", "Middle 60%", "Top 20%"))

cagr_data <- bind_rows(income_cagr, hdb_cagr) %>%
  mutate(
    Income_Group = factor(Income_Group, levels = c("Bottom 20%", "Middle 60%", "Top 20%")),
    Sub_Period   = factor(Sub_Period,   levels = c("2000–2010", "2010–2020", "2020–2025")),
    Measure      = factor(Measure,      levels = c("Real Income", "HDB Resale Price"))
  )

ggplot(cagr_data, aes(x = Sub_Period, y = CAGR, fill = Measure)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(0.65), width = 0.55) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.4, color = "grey40") +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(round(CAGR, 1), "%"),
        vjust = ifelse(CAGR >= 0, -0.4, 1.3)),
    position = position_dodge(0.65),
    size = 2.8, fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~Income_Group, ncol = 3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = col_measure, name = NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%"),
    breaks = seq(-2, 10, by = 2)
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "CAGR of real income vs HDB resale prices by sub-period",
    subtitle = "Three periods: 2000–2010, 2010–2020, 2020–2025",
    x = NULL, y = "CAGR (%)",
    caption  = "Sources: SingStat, HDB / data.gov.sg"
  ) +
  base_theme +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 0.5))
```
:::
